Dear Valued Guests,
Please be advised that on Tuesday, 05/19/2026, our property will be conducting routine pest control services in this room of the hotel. The time of service will be from 1pm - 6pm.
This service is mandatory and not optional. Rescheduling is also not optional for this type of necessary work. In order to maintain a clean, safe, and comfortable environment for all guests, the licensed technicians will require access to each room with a member of staff that will be accompanying them during the process.
To ensure a smooth and efficient service process, we kindly ask that you:
• Remove all pets from your room prior to service. Pets must be temporarily vacated from the room during service.
• Follow the Service Guide Checklist attached to this notice to properly prepare your room before the scheduled service time.
It is required that you leave the room for during the time of service to help with this process.
We appreciate your cooperation as we take proactive steps to maintain the comfort and quality of your stay. If you have any questions, please contact the front desk.
Thank you,
Extended Stay America - Bothell West Management
extended
STAY
AMERICA®

PRIOR TO PEST SERVICES CHECK LIST
MPORTANT NOTE: it is normal to see dead and dying cockroaches for several days following this type of treatment It is recommended that dead cockroaches be vacuumed up following treatment as these may serve as a source of allergens, as well as a food source for dermestid beetles feeding on the dead insects. Ventilate the area before re-entry after treatment, especially if an odor is present.
COMPLETED
PREPARATION REQUIREMENTS

KITCHEN

Remove all items from all kitchen and food storage areas

Place all opened food items in the refrigerator or in sealed plastic bags or tubs; these sealed items may be placed on table tops and should be covered

Remove items from floors to allow a clear, obstacle-free path for Technicians

Empty trash cans and do not replace bags until after the service

Vacuum cabinets and shelving to remove food debris

Clean and put away dishes and clean kitchen counters, sink, and stove/range top

BATHROOMS

Remove all items from medicine cabinets, storage cabinets, and drawers

Place personal items (toothbrushes, hair brushes, cosmetics, toiletries, etc.) in sealed plastic bags

Items removed from cabinets, etc may be placed in the tub during service to keep floor areas clear for service

BEDROOM

Remove all items from the closet floor and shelving

Remove drawers from dressers, nightstands, etc

Remove items from any cabinetry, entertainment centers, etc.

Items may be placed on the bed during service

Keep floors clear to allow for an unimpeded path for Technicians

OTHER

All people and animals should remain out of the treatment areas during and after treatment until all surfaces are dry; a minimum of 3 hours is advised

Pet food dishes should be emptied, clean, and covered

record.text:
#3wordchallenge - Emerald • Clock • Echo
Emerald Clock Echo
Somewhere between midnight and memory, she stopped aging.
#Clockpunk #GothicPortrait #DarkFantasy #SurrealArt #EmeraldAesthetic #AIart #AIchallenge #aiartcommunity #aiartist #GenAI #GenerativeArt #SynthArt #BuilderofNightmares

images.alt:
A stylized gothic girl stands centered in front of a massive blurred clock face whose oversized Roman numerals loom faintly through pale fog and drifting debris. The entire scene is washed in cool emerald-green tones mixed with soft grayscale whites, creating an eerie dreamlike atmosphere. Her hair is messy and sharply layered, blowing outward in uneven directions as though caught in invisible static or time distortion. One eye is hidden behind heavy bangs while the visible eye stares directly toward the viewer through oversized circular glasses with green-tinted lenses.
She wears a distressed black jacket covered in frayed edges, stitched textures, and scattered buttons layered over a dark cropped top and short skirt. A black choker circles her neck alongside a thin necklace with a hanging pendant. Her outfit appears partially dissolving into smoky particles and torn fragments around the sleeves and hemline, as if her body is slowly unraveling into ash or ink.
The background clock dominates the composition without fully coming into focus, reinforcing the feeling that she exists inside suspended time. Fine scratches, floating dust, and painterly distortions scatter across the image, adding motion and instability. The portrait blends gothic fashion, surreal fantasy illustration, and soft horror aesthetics into a melancholic image that feels both delicate and haunted.
Keywords:
gothic girl, emerald tones, clock face, surreal portrait, messy hair, oversized glasses, dissolving fabric, monochrome aesthetic, dark fashion, eerie atmosphere, painterly texture, suspended time
AI-generated; use is permitted for non-commercial creative projects. MidJourney, FLUX.1-schnell model via Perchance, Artspace.ai, REVE, Echoform™.

images.alt:
A cleanly designed 4x4 emotional expression grid titled “MY CUTE ↔ CRAZY SCALE” fills the frame against a soft off-white background. Each square contains a tightly framed portrait of the same elderly man photographed in consistent lighting and composition. He is bald with a long white beard, pale skin, and oversized round black glasses that dominate his face. The portraits progress gradually from gentle warmth to complete manic instability as the viewer moves left to right and top to bottom.
The first row begins with soft, innocent expressions: calm eye contact, shy smiles, playful winks, and teasing tongue-out gestures. The second row grows more energetic and emotionally volatile, with exaggerated open-mouth excitement, chaotic playfulness, and forced unstable smiles. By the third row, the emotional intensity spikes sharply. His eyes widen dramatically, facial muscles tense, and expressions swing between ecstatic laughter and visible psychological strain. The final row descends fully into theatrical madness, featuring frozen chaotic stares, unnerving grins, bulging eyes, and a final portrait where the man appears to be screaming directly at the viewer in complete emotional collapse.
The handwritten-style labels beneath each image reinforce the progression, transforming the grid into a humorous but strangely unsettling emotional spectrum chart. Despite the absurd escalation, the photographic consistency remains precise throughout, giving the entire piece the feeling of a scientific personality study slowly losing control.
Keywords:
expression grid, emotional progression, elderly man, round glasses, white beard, portrait study, escalating chaos, humorous insanity, character sheet, facial expressions, absurd comedy, psychological spectrum
AI-generated; use is permitted for non-commercial creative projects. MidJourney, FLUX.1-schnell model via Perchance, Artspace.ai, REVE, Echoform™.

external.description: It's a controversial executive/ DOJ settlement using existing claims authority, not a traditional congressional "slush fund," and its legality is actively disputed.  What it actually is (as of mid-May 2026) Trump (and family/entities) filed a $10 billion lawsuit against the IRS over the 2019 leak of his tax returns. Reports indicate he's dropping/withdrawing it in exchange for the DOJ/Trump administration creating a ~$1.7 billion (or $1.776B) "anti-weaponization" compensation fund. The fund draws from existing administrative claims/settlement pots (not new congressional appropriation). A commission (appointed by the administration) would review and pay claims from individuals alleging harm from Biden-era DOJ/FBI "lawfare," explicitly including many of the ~1,600 Jan. 6 defendants (most already pardoned). Trump himself reportedly can't directly claim, but allies/entities might. It's framed as redressing perceived selective prosecution.  This emerged rapidly after Trump's return to office, alongside broad Jan. 6 pardons/commutations.  Why critics call it a "slush fund" and question legality Self-dealing/collusive lawsuit: Trump is effectively suing an agency he controls (as President). Democrats argue this lacks a true "case or controversy" under Article III, making any settlement a sham to raid the Treasury. House Democrats filed an amicus brief to block it, citing violation of Congress's power of the purse. Taxpayer money to Jan. 6 participants: Paying people convicted of (or charged with) offenses tied to the Capitol attack is highly inflammatory. Critics (Raskin, Warren, etc.) invoke the 14th Amendment's bar on assuming obligations "incurred in aid of insurrection." Lack of oversight: A presidential commission dispensing ~$1.7B with broad discretion, low transparency, and no direct congressional approval looks like patronage. Precedent/ethics: Presidents have broad settlement authority via DOJ, but this scale and direct tie to a personal suit + political allies is unusual.  Counterarguments for why it's (arguably) legal Executive settlement power: DOJ routinely settles claims against the government using judgment/claims funds authorized by Congress (e.g., Federal Tort Claims Act, administrative settlements). If framed as redressing wrongful prosecutions or leaks, it fits existing mechanisms. Pardons context: Trump already issued broad clemency for most Jan. 6 cases. Refunds of fines/restitution or compensation for "wrongful" targeting follow from that (DOJ has supported some restitution refunds post-pardon). Political reciprocity: Both parties have long used DOJ/civil settlements, victim funds, and pardons with financial relief. Critics' outrage is selective; similar "weaponization" complaints existed under prior admins. Not unlimited: It uses pre-existing pots (~$1.7B from administrative funds), not a blank congressional check. Courts would decide challenges.  Bottom line: It's "legal" in the narrow sense that the executive branch has wide latitude on settlements and clemency consequences, especially with a friendly DOJ. But it's vulnerable to lawsuits (ongoing Democratic challenges on jurisdiction, appropriations clause, 14th Amendment). A court could block or narrow it. Whether it's wise or ethical is separate—paying participants in a riot that injured officers and cost taxpayers millions is radioactive, even if you believe many cases were overcharged. This is raw politics via bureaucracy, not hidden illegality. Expect court fights and congressional oversight attempts.
external.title: How is Trump’s J6 slush fund legal?!?

lol, Ben Thompson has given up on tech companies winning data center fights on messaging and thinks they should just start doing UBI themselves to grease the wheels.

images.alt:
The third thing tech could do — and this is where I think the answer lies — is simply start paying people off directly. Data center builders and operators already stress the economic benefits to local municipalities, of course, and Meta to its credit actually does quite a bit more than that, including workforce training. At the end of the day, however, I don’t think any of this is concrete enough to offset people’s anxiety about AI, which makes up the receptive soil in which the seed of misinformation lands and takes root. And, I would add, all of these benefits assume a level of trust in local governments, in terms of benefits trickling down to citizens, that is rapidly eroding.

Instead, the most obvious solution is the most crass: simply start giving people money. If data centers are a resource for our AI future, then start paying people for that resource. If that data center up the road weren’t sold to my neighbors based on amorphous tax benefits that my local government may or may not spend appropriately, but rather were to result in a check in the mailbox every year, I suspect you could get a lot more people on board!

Just to put some numbers on this, the data center up the road was expected to be 1.6 GW, which could generate around $3 billion in annual operator revenue. DeForest, the village it was to be built in, has around 11,500 people. You could pay every person in DeForest $10,000 a year for 3.8% of annual revenue grossed by the data center — I bet that proposal would have been approved, and I bet that the operator could very easily pass those costs on to the actual data center users (it also highlights how relatively pathetic QTS’s $50 million commitment was).

I do get how ridiculous this sounds, but ridiculous is how we do things in America. We kind-of have universal health insurance — it’s called the Emergency Medical Treatment and Labor Act, which mandates care for anyone appearing in an emergency room. We have widespread welfare, it’s called Social Security Disability or Section 8 Housing or SNAP. And, once Big Tech starts paying people off for data centers, we’ll suddenly have AI-derived Universal Basic Income, via the most ridiculous mechanism possible, that both gets things done in an unfair kludgy way and lets us pretend it doesn’t exist — it’s just how we do things!

It’s also the reason this all works. Providing UBI via data center placement will, in the end, get data centers built, which will generate the economic growth that will make everyone better off. Yes, right now a lot of AI’s value is predicated on cutting below the line; what America does best, however, is focus relentlessly on the top line, doing whatever is necessary to make it go up and to the right.

external.description: O chef baiano Jônatas Bomfim será um dos destaques do I Encontro da Estratégia Intersetorial para a Redução de Perdas e Desperdício de Alimentos no Brasil, que acontece nos dias 26 e 27 de maio de 2026, no Teatro SESC/SENAC Pelourinho, em Salvador. O convite parte do Ministério do Desenvolvimento e Assistência Social, Família e Combate à Fome (MDS), órgão responsável pela articulação nacional de políticas públicas voltadas à segurança alimentar, em parceria com o Sesc. O encontro integra ainda o IV Encontro Nacional de Bancos de Alimentos, consolidando-se como um dos principais fóruns estratégicos do país sobre o tema.

Com respostas bem estruturadas, IA pode gerar conteúdos plausíveis, mas imprecisos, reforçando a importância do pensamento crítico no uso da tecnologia A popularização de ferramentas de inteligência artificial como o ChatGPT tem…

external.description: Com respostas bem estruturadas, IA pode gerar conteúdos plausíveis, mas imprecisos, reforçando a importância do pensamento crítico no uso da tecnologia A popularização de ferramentas de inteligência artificial como o ChatGPT tem transformado a forma como as pessoas buscam informações, estudam e tomam decisões no dia a dia. Em determinados contextos, especialmente quando não há dados suficientes ou quando o tema é muito específico, esses sistemas podem gerar conteúdos que parecem corretos, mas não são necessariamente verdadeiros. Esse comportamento, conhecido como “alucinação”, já foi reconhecido como uma limitação dos modelos de linguagem.
